visa_policy.notes: Bosnia and Herzegovina's visa policy is governed by the Law on Movement and Stay of Aliens and Asylum, and the Decision on Visas. Citizens of EU/Schengen member states may enter using national ID cards instead of passports. Bosnia and Herzegovina does not operate an e-visa or ETA system. No official nationality-enumerated list was accessible directly from mvp.gov.ba due to certificate errors; data compiled from official BiH embassy websites (Norway, USA, Belgium) which are government-operated embassy domains. Diplomatic/official passport exemptions apply separately for Algeria, Armenia, China, Cuba, Egypt, Indonesia, Iran, Jordan, Kazakhstan, Morocco, Pakistan, Saudi Arabia, Tunisia, and Ukraine — these are classified under conditional_access as passport_type entries. Kuwait had visa-free access but Bosnia and Herzegovina reintroduced the visa requirement for Kuwaiti nationals effective October 1, 2025, as part of EU alignment obligations. Bahrain, Oman, and Saudi Arabia have a SEASONAL visa-free waiver only (June 1 – September 30 annually, up to 30 days per trip) — this is NOT a year-round 90-day regime and is classified under conditional_access. UAE has a separate permanent bilateral MoU (November 2023) granting year-round 90-day visa-free access. UK-issued visas and residence permits do NOT qualify for the credential-based waiver. Bosnia and Herzegovina committed to terminating one visa-free agreement per year in 2025–2027 as part of EU accession harmonization.

conditional_access.conditions: Nationals of countries with whom Bosnia and Herzegovina has a visa regime may enter without a BiH visa for up to 30 days if they hold a valid multiple-entry visa issued by a Schengen Agreement signatory state. The visa must be valid for at least 30 days beyond the date of entry. One continuous stay cannot exceed 30 days.

Nationals of countries with whom Bosnia and Herzegovina has a visa regime may enter without a BiH visa for up to 30 days if they hold a valid residence permit issued by a Schengen Agreement signatory state. The permit must be valid for at least 30 days beyond the date of entry. One continuous stay cannot exceed 30 days.

Nationals of countries with whom Bosnia and Herzegovina has a visa regime may enter without a BiH visa for up to 30 days if they hold a valid multiple-entry visa issued by an EU member state. The visa must be valid for at least 30 days beyond the date of entry. One continuous stay cannot exceed 30 days.

Nationals of countries with whom Bosnia and Herzegovina has a visa regime may enter without a BiH visa for up to 30 days if they hold a valid residence permit issued by an EU member state. The permit must be valid for at least 30 days beyond the date of entry. One continuous stay cannot exceed 30 days.

Nationals of countries with whom Bosnia and Herzegovina has a visa regime may enter without a BiH visa for up to 30 days if they hold a valid multiple-entry US visa. The visa must be valid for at least 30 days beyond the date of entry. UK-issued visas explicitly DO NOT qualify (post-Brexit). One continuous stay cannot exceed 30 days.

Nationals of countries with whom Bosnia and Herzegovina has a visa regime may enter without a BiH visa for up to 30 days if they hold a valid US permanent residence permit. The permit must be valid for at least 30 days beyond the date of entry. One continuous stay cannot exceed 30 days.

Citizens of Bahrain may enter Bosnia and Herzegovina visa-free ONLY during the tourist season: June 1 to September 30 each calendar year, for a maximum of 30 days per trip. This is an annual seasonal waiver adopted by the Council of Ministers; it is NOT a year-round arrangement. Outside this window, a visa is required.

Citizens of Oman may enter Bosnia and Herzegovina visa-free ONLY during the tourist season: June 1 to September 30 each calendar year, for a maximum of 30 days per trip. This is an annual seasonal waiver adopted by the Council of Ministers; it is NOT a year-round arrangement. Outside this window, a visa is required.

Citizens of Saudi Arabia may enter Bosnia and Herzegovina visa-free ONLY during the tourist season: June 1 to September 30 each calendar year, for a maximum of 30 days per trip. This is an annual seasonal waiver adopted by the Council of Ministers; it is NOT a year-round arrangement. Outside this window, a visa is required. Note: Saudi Arabia also has diplomatic/service passport exemptions separately.
Holders of Algerian diplomatic passports are exempt from visa requirements for Bosnia and Herzegovina. Ordinary passport holders require a visa.
Holders of Armenian diplomatic passports are exempt from visa requirements for Bosnia and Herzegovina. Ordinary passport holders require a visa.

Holders of Chinese diplomatic and official/service passports are exempt from visa requirements for Bosnia and Herzegovina. Ordinary/business-civil passport holders are separately visa-free per bilateral agreement.
Holders of Cuban diplomatic and official/service passports are exempt from visa requirements for Bosnia and Herzegovina. Ordinary passport holders require a visa.
Holders of Egyptian diplomatic and official/service passports are exempt from visa requirements for Bosnia and Herzegovina. Ordinary passport holders require a visa.
Holders of Indonesian diplomatic and official/service passports are exempt from visa requirements for Bosnia and Herzegovina. Ordinary passport holders require a visa.
Holders of Iranian diplomatic and official/service passports are exempt from visa requirements for Bosnia and Herzegovina. Ordinary passport holders require a visa.
Holders of Jordanian diplomatic and official/service passports are exempt from visa requirements for Bosnia and Herzegovina. Ordinary passport holders require a visa.
Holders of Kazakhstani diplomatic and official/service passports are exempt from visa requirements for Bosnia and Herzegovina. Ordinary passport holders require a visa.
Holders of Moroccan diplomatic and official/service passports are exempt from visa requirements for Bosnia and Herzegovina. Ordinary passport holders require a visa.
Holders of Pakistani diplomatic and official/service passports are exempt from visa requirements for Bosnia and Herzegovina. Ordinary passport holders require a visa.

Holders of Russian diplomatic and official/service passports are exempt from visa requirements for Bosnia and Herzegovina. Ordinary passport holders are separately visa-free for up to 30 days per 60-day period.

Holders of Saudi Arabian diplomatic and official/service passports are exempt from visa requirements for Bosnia and Herzegovina. Ordinary passport holders have only a seasonal visa waiver (June 1 - September 30, 30 days).
Holders of Tunisian diplomatic and official/service passports are exempt from visa requirements for Bosnia and Herzegovina. Ordinary passport holders require a visa.

Holders of Ukrainian diplomatic and official/service passports are exempt from visa requirements for Bosnia and Herzegovina. Ordinary passport holders are separately visa-free for up to 30 days per 60-day period.

cbi.notes: Bosnia and Herzegovina does not operate a formal citizenship-by-investment (CBI) program. The Law on Citizenship of Bosnia and Herzegovina (1997) contains an Article 15 provision for 'exceptional naturalization' of persons considered 'of particular benefit to Bosnia and Herzegovina,' which can include investors and business owners making significant economic contributions or creating substantial employment. However, this is a discretionary provision requiring Council of Ministers approval, not a structured CBI program with defined investment thresholds. No official government website advertises or administers a CBI program.

programs.name: Temporary Residence Based on Real Estate Ownership
programs.notes: Foreign nationals who own real estate in Bosnia and Herzegovina may apply for a temporary residence permit. No minimum property value is specified in law. The applicant must also demonstrate a 'real and lasting connection' to Bosnia and Herzegovina (e.g., origin, children's education, pension receipt, or investment in economic activities). Issued for up to 1 year, renewable. After 5 consecutive years of temporary residence, permanent residence may be applied for. Administrative fee: 150 BAM for issuance, 200 BAM for permanent residence.

programs.name: Temporary Residence Based on Company/Business Establishment (Work Without Work Permit)
programs.notes: Founders of a company or enterprise registered in Bosnia and Herzegovina may apply for temporary residence on the basis of work without a work permit (business founder category). Minimum registered capital in FBiH entity is 1,000 BAM (approx. EUR 511) for a d.o.o. company. No higher investment threshold is set for this residence category. Temporary residence valid up to 1 year, renewable. Path to permanent residence after 5 uninterrupted years of temporary residence.

agreements: CEFTA (Central European Free Trade Agreement) — trade liberalization, not freedom of movement

validation_notes: REMOVED Kuwait (KWT) from visa_free: BiH Council of Ministers reintroduced visa requirement for Kuwaiti nationals effective October 1, 2025, as part of EU accession harmonization. Kuwait was incorrectly listed as visa_free (90 days) in the prior record.

RECLASSIFIED Bahrain (BHR) from visa_free to conditional_access (basis=seasonal): The prior record incorrectly showed Bahrain as having a year-round 90-day visa-free regime. In fact, it is a SEASONAL waiver only (June 1 - September 30, max 30 days per trip), annually renewed by Council of Ministers decision. The prior notes even stated '30-day seasonal' but the classification was wrong.
RECLASSIFIED Oman (OMN) from visa_free to conditional_access (basis=seasonal): Same reason as Bahrain — the regime is seasonal only (June 1 - September 30, 30 days), not year-round 90 days.

RECLASSIFIED Saudi Arabia (SAU) from visa_free to conditional_access (basis=seasonal): Same reason — seasonal waiver only (June 1 - September 30, 30 days). Saudi Arabia retains diplomatic/service passport exemption in conditional_access.

UPDATED UAE (ARE) source_url: Changed from Norway embassy source to official UAE MoFA announcement confirming the November 6, 2023 bilateral MoU; updated notes to clarify this is a permanent year-round arrangement.
REMOVED the redundant consolidated conditional_access entry that duplicated the individual credential entries with issuer='Schengen|EU|US' — these are now expressed as separate individual entries.
UPDATED Russia notes: Added note about EU pressure to cancel the regime; confirmed the ordinary passport visa-free (30 days/60-day period) remains in force as of June 2026.
CORRECTED visa_on_arrival notes: Added '10 designated land border crossings' to the list of locations where exceptional border visas can be issued.
gaps: The primary official MFA visa page (mvp.gov.ba/en/vize) and the Decision on Visas PDF were inaccessible due to SSL certificate errors; the nationality list was compiled from official BiH embassy websites (Norway, USA, Belgium) and Wikipedia's policy article.
Kuwait's exact removal date (October 1, 2025) confirmed by multiple secondary sources referencing the Council of Ministers decision; not independently verified from mvp.gov.ba (SSL blocked).

Bahrain/Oman/Saudi Arabia seasonal waiver for 2026 (June 1 - September 30 2026) has not yet been independently confirmed from an official announcement for the 2026 season; the pattern has been annual renewal but this should be checked near June 2026.
Some embassy lists (Brussels vs. Norway) differ on Singapore (diplomatic-only vs. general visa-free); discrepancy noted in Singapore entry.
No e-visa system exists for Bosnia and Herzegovina as of June 2026.
No ETA system exists for Bosnia and Herzegovina.
No digital nomad visa, startup visa, or fast-track talent scheme found on any official BiH government source.
No CBI program exists; the Article 15 'exceptional naturalization' provision is discretionary and has no official program page.

Minimum investment amounts for business-based residence are not officially published; the 1,000 BAM figure is the minimum share capital for company registration, not an immigration-specific investment requirement.
Dual citizenship policy for naturalized citizens not definitively confirmed from official sources reviewed.
Bosnia and Herzegovina has EU candidate status; not yet a member of EU/EEA or Schengen Area.
UK-issued visas and residence permits explicitly DO NOT qualify for the Schengen/EU/US credential rule (post-Brexit).
No OCI (Overseas Citizen of India) or similar special-status cardholder rule found on any official BiH government source.
Exact max_stay_days for diplomatic/service passport exemptions not stated on official sources reviewed.
Belarus diplomatic/service passport exemption reported by some aggregators but not confirmed on official BiH embassy pages reviewed — not recorded.

Qatar's visa-free status (year-round 90 days) confirmed via secondary sources; it remains part of BiH's permanent bilateral agreements and was not affected by the EU harmonization measure that removed Kuwait.

Hong Kong SAR, Macao SAR, and Taiwan (ROC) travel document holders — Wikipedia lists some as visa-free but this could not be confirmed from official BiH embassy pages reviewed; not recorded as separate entries.

outbound_official.notes: Official Ministry of Foreign Affairs of Bosnia and Herzegovina visa page (mvp.gov.ba/bs/vize) lists per-country visa requirements for BiH citizens. "Obicna biometrijska putna isprava NE" = ordinary biometric passport, no visa required. Since BiH has issued only biometric passports since 2014, all 65 visa-free entries apply to current BiH ordinary passport holders. Russia: visa-free by bilateral agreement up to 30 days per 60-day period (confirmed in page text). Belarus: NE but limited to 5-day transit via Minsk airport. Tajikistan and Uzbekistan: NE for tourist stays up to 30 days. UAE: NE confirmed by bilateral MoU (90 days per 180-day period). Cambodia: visa obtainable on arrival or via e-visa. Indonesia: visa on arrival for tourism (30 days, ~USD 35). Nepal: visa on arrival. Cuba: tourist card required (not traditional visa).
